---
title: "Client Report - What's in a Name?"
subtitle: "Unit 1 Task 2"
author: "[STUDENT NAME]"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
df = pl.read_csv("https://posit.byui.edu/content/0d43d66f-d3b7-4c43-8c36-52e3ed69e7f1/names_year_csv.csv")

# Keep only the columns we need for this report.
names = (
  df
  .select(["name", "year", "Total"])
  .with_columns(
    pl.col("name").str.to_titlecase(),
    pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int64),
    pl.col("Total").cast(pl.Int64)
  )
  .rename({"Total": "count"})
)
```

## QUESTION 1

<!-- Make sure at least one of your charts includes reference marks!! -->

__How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.__ 

For this report, I used the name Vincent and birth year 2005. In 2005, Vincent was given 3,737 times, while its historical peak was 6,162 in 1962. That means my birth-year usage is substantial but clearly below the mid-century high, suggesting the name remained popular but had declined from its peak.

```{python}
# Q1
my_name = "Vincent"
birth_year = 2005

q1 = (
  names
  .filter(pl.col("name") == my_name)
  .sort("year")
)

birth_row = q1.filter(pl.col("year") == birth_year)
birth_count = int(birth_row["count"][0]) if birth_row.height > 0 else 0

peak_row = q1.sort("count", descending=True).head(1)
peak_year = int(peak_row["year"][0])
peak_count = int(peak_row["count"][0])

ggplot(q1, aes(x="year", y="count")) + \
  geom_line(color="#1f77b4", size=1.0) + \
  geom_point(data=q1.filter(pl.col("year") == birth_year), color="#d62728", size=3) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Historical U.S. Usage of the Name {my_name}",
    subtitle=f"Birth year ({birth_year}) count: {birth_count}; all-time peak: {peak_count} in {peak_year}",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()


```


## QUESTION 2

__Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?__  

I chose **Anakin** from *Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace* (1999). 
The chart shows very low usage before 1999, followed by a clear jump and a higher sustained level afterward. 
This timing strongly suggests the movie release affected how often the name was chosen.

```{python}
# Q2
movie_name = "Anakin"
release_year = 1999

q2 = (
  names
  .filter(pl.col("name") == movie_name)
  .sort("year")
)

label_df = pl.DataFrame({
  "x": [release_year + 2],
  "y": [int(q2["count"].max() * 0.85)],
  "label": ["Movie release (1999)"]
})

ggplot(q2, aes(x="year", y="count")) + \
  geom_line(color="#2ca02c", size=1.1) + \
  geom_vline(xintercept=release_year, linetype="dashed", color="#d62728", size=1) + \
  geom_text(
    data=label_df,
    mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", label="label"),
    color="#d62728",
    size=10
  ) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Usage of the Name {movie_name} Over Time",
    subtitle="Reference mark shows the movie release year",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()


```

## QUESTION 3

__Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?__  

From 1920 to 2000, Mary starts far above the other three names and then drops sharply over time. Martha, Peter, and Paul also decline overall, 
but with smaller counts and less dramatic slopes than Mary. 
The main trend is broad long-run decline across all four names,
 with Mary showing the biggest historical shift.

```{python}
# Q3
target_names = ["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"]

q3 = (
  names
  .filter(
    pl.col("name").is_in(target_names),
    pl.col("year") >= 1920,
    pl.col("year") <= 2000
  )
  .sort(["name", "year"])
)

ggplot(q3, aes(x="year", y="count", color="name")) + \
  geom_line(size=1) + \
  labs(
    title="Christian Name Usage in the U.S. (1920-2000)",
    subtitle="Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name",
    color="Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()


```
<!-- Make sure at least one of your charts includes reference marks!! -->
